era_context: The early 15th century is marked by the opening of the Council of Constance (1414–1418) to address the Western Schism and church reform, the consolidation of Ming Dynasty power under the Yongle Emperor, Zheng He's treasure voyages at their height, the Hundred Years' War between England and France, and the Ottoman Empire's consolidation under Mehmed I. The Inca Empire is expanding under Viracocha Inca (traditional dating disputed).
geographic_coverage_gaps: Interior South American societies beyond Inca sphere

events.title: Council of Constance opens; major ecclesiastical assembly to address Western Schism
events.description: The Council of Constance opens on November 5, 1414, as one of the largest and most important ecclesiastical assemblies of the medieval period. The council is convened to address the Western Schism (1378–1417), which has divided the Catholic Church among competing papal claimants. The council brings together bishops, theologians, princes, and representatives from across Christendom. Major issues include the resolution of the schism, the suppression of heresy (particularly the teachings of John Wycliffe and Jan Hus), and church reform. The council will run until 1418 and will result in the election of Pope Martin V, effectively ending the schism. The council also condemns John Hus to death (1415), an action that will have major political consequences.
key_figures: Emperor Sigismund (Holy Roman Emperor, convener)
Pope John XXIII (one of three papal claimants, initially supporter)
Cardinal Pierre d'Ailly (council leader)
John Hus (Bohemian reformer, tried and condemned)

sources.name: Council of Constance records and decrees (contemporary documentation)
Ulrich of Richenthal, Chronicle of the Council of Constance (written 1420s, near-contemporary)

events.certainty_note: The opening date of the Council of Constance is precisely documented. The council's proceedings are extensively recorded in contemporary sources.

events.title: Zheng He's treasure voyages; second voyage reaches East Africa and Arabian Peninsula
events.region: Indian Ocean, East Africa, Arabian Peninsula, Ming China
events.description: Admiral Zheng He's second major voyage (1407–1409, with some sources extending to 1414) demonstrates Ming naval power and diplomatic reach. The massive fleet of treasure ships carries envoys, gifts, and trade goods across the Indian Ocean to East Africa, the Arabian Peninsula, and the Persian Gulf. The voyages establish tributary relationships and gather exotic goods and animals for the Ming court. By 1414, the voyages are at their peak in terms of scale and geographic reach. The fleet includes over 300 ships with crews exceeding 27,000. These voyages represent the height of Ming maritime power before the program is curtailed in the 1430s.
key_figures: Zheng He (Admiral, Muslim eunuch commanding the fleet)
Yongle Emperor (Ming ruler sponsoring the voyages)
Ma Huan (fleet official, chronicler of the voyages)

sources.name: Ming Shilu (Ming Veritable Records, official court records)
Ma Huan, Yingya Shenglan (Overall Survey of the Ocean's Shores, written 1416–1422, participant account)

events.certainty_note: Zheng He's voyages are extensively documented in Ming records. The second voyage is dated 1407–1409 in most sources, though some extend it to 1414. Assigned here based on broader voyage timeline.

events.title: Yongle Emperor consolidates Ming power; Forbidden City construction continues
events.description: The Yongle Emperor (r. 1402–1424) continues to consolidate Ming power and oversee the construction of the Forbidden City, begun in 1406. By 1414, the palace complex is nearing completion and represents a massive investment in state power and imperial authority. The Yongle reign marks the height of early Ming power and cultural achievement. The emperor has firmly consolidated control after the Jingnan Rebellion (1399–1402) and is pursuing an expansionist foreign policy, including the sponsorship of Zheng He's treasure voyages.
key_figures: Yongle Emperor (Zhu Di, Ming ruler)
Cai Xin (chief eunuch, major influence on court)
Architects and engineers (names largely unrecorded)

sources.name: Archaeological evidence from the Forbidden City (excavation and architectural analysis)

events.certainty_note: The Forbidden City's construction timeline is well-documented. By 1414, the complex is substantially complete.

events.title: Hundred Years' War continues; English campaigns in France under Henry V
events.region: Kingdom of France, Kingdom of England
events.description: The Hundred Years' War (1337–1453) continues with English forces under King Henry V conducting campaigns in France. By 1414, Henry V is preparing for major military operations that will culminate in the Battle of Agincourt (1415). The war is driven by English claims to the French throne and competition for control of French territories. The conflict has devastated large parts of France and has become a defining feature of both English and French politics. Henry V's aggressive prosecution of the war will lead to major English victories and territorial gains in the early 1420s.
key_figures: Henry V (King of England, military commander)
Charles VI (King of France, mentally unstable)
Duke of Burgundy (powerful French noble, sometimes allied with England)
Joan of Arc (future French military leader, not yet prominent in 1414)

sources.name: English royal chronicles and records (contemporary or near-contemporary)
French royal chronicles and records (contemporary or near-contemporary)

events.certainty_note: The Hundred Years' War is extensively documented in both English and French sources. The year 1414 falls within a period of active English military campaigns.

events.title: Mehmed I consolidates Ottoman power; Interregnum ends
events.description: Mehmed I (r. 1413–1421) consolidates his position as Ottoman Sultan following the Interregnum (1402–1413). By 1414, he has secured his authority against rival claimants and is expanding Ottoman control in the Balkans and Anatolia. The Ottoman Empire is becoming the dominant power in the Eastern Mediterranean and Balkans. Mehmed I's reign marks a period of relative stability and growth after the chaos of the Interregnum. The Ottomans face challenges from Anatolian beyliks and external pressure from Venice and Hungary, but Mehmed's strong leadership enables consolidation.
key_figures: Mehmed I (Ottoman Sultan, r. 1413–1421)
Ibrahim Pasha (Grand Vizier, chief administrator)
Rival claimants and regional governors (various)

sources.name: Ottoman court records and chronicles (contemporary or near-contemporary)
Ashikpashazade, Tevarih-i Al-i Osman (History of the House of Osman, written 15th century)

events.certainty_note: Mehmed I's reign and consolidation are well-documented in Ottoman sources. The year 1414 falls clearly within his reign.

events.title: Inca Empire under Viracocha Inca; territorial expansion in the Andes
events.description: Viracocha Inca (traditional dates 1410–1438) rules the Inca Empire during a period of consolidation and expansion. By 1414, he has secured control over the central Andes and is expanding Inca territory through military campaigns and diplomatic alliances. The Inca state is becoming increasingly centralized and militarized. Viracocha Inca is traditionally succeeded by Pachacuti, though the exact dating and nature of this transition is contested. The Inca chronology is highly uncertain due to the absence of written records and reliance on oral traditions recorded by Spanish colonizers.
key_figures: Viracocha Inca (Inca ruler, traditional dates 1410–1438)
Pachacuti (successor, traditional dates 1438–1471)

sources.name: Quipucamayoc oral traditions (recorded by Spanish chroniclers in 16th century)
Pedro Cieza de León, Crónica del Perú (written 1553, based on interviews and oral tradition)
Archaeological evidence from Inca sites (stratigraphy, radiocarbon dating)

events.certainty_note: Inca chronology is highly contested. Spanish chroniclers recorded oral traditions decades or centuries after events. Radiocarbon dating and archaeological evidence suggest Viracocha Inca's reign occurred in the 15th century, but exact dates are uncertain.

events.title: Venice maintains Mediterranean dominance; commercial and naval power
events.description: Venice continues to dominate Mediterranean trade and maintains its position as the leading maritime power in the region. By 1414, Venice has achieved commercial supremacy through control of trade routes to the East, particularly spices and luxury goods. The Venetian Republic is wealthy and politically influential, though it faces increasing competition from the Ottoman Empire and challenges from other European powers. Venice's dominance will eventually be challenged by the Ottoman conquest of Constantinople (1453) and by the opening of Atlantic trade routes.
key_figures: Venetian Doge (leadership of the Venetian Republic)
Merchant families (Contarini, Morosini, Dandolo, etc.)
Venetian naval commanders

sources.name: Venetian state archives and chronicles (contemporary documentation)
Merchant account books and trade records (contemporary documentation)

events.certainty_note: Venetian dominance in Mediterranean trade is well-documented in contemporary sources. The year 1414 falls within a period of Venetian commercial supremacy.
cross_references: 1453-001 (Ottoman conquest of Constantinople affects Venetian trade)

events.title: Portuguese exploration of African coast; Prince Henry the Navigator's patronage
events.description: Prince Henry the Navigator of Portugal continues to sponsor expeditions along the West African coast. By 1414, Portuguese explorers are beginning to venture beyond previously known territories. Henry's court at Sagres becomes a center for cartography, navigation, and geographic knowledge. These voyages are driven by commercial interests (gold, spices, enslaved people), religious zeal (converting Muslims), and geographic curiosity. The Portuguese exploration program will eventually lead to the rounding of the Cape of Good Hope (1488) and the sea route to India (1498).
key_figures: Prince Henry the Navigator (Portuguese royal, sponsor of exploration)
Portuguese sea captains and navigators (names often unrecorded for early voyages)
West African rulers (encountering Portuguese traders)

sources.name: Portuguese royal records and chronicles (contemporary or near-contemporary)
Gomes Eanes de Zurara, Crónica da Guiné (Chronicle of Guinea, written 1450s, based on court records)

events.certainty_note: Portuguese exploration in 1414 is attested in general terms, though specific voyages and dates are often vague in sources. The broader program is confirmed; individual expedition details are less certain.

disconfirming_evidence: The dating of Inca rulers is highly contested. Traditional chronologies place Viracocha Inca at 1410–1438, but modern scholars debate these dates based on archaeological evidence and reanalysis of Spanish chronicles. Zheng He's second voyage is dated 1407–1409 in most sources, though some extend it to 1414; the assignment to 1414 reflects this ambiguity. The significance of Portuguese exploration in 1414 is less dramatic than in later years; the major conquest of Ceuta occurs in 1415, and the rounding of Cape Bojador is traditionally dated to 1434.
historiographic_note: The year 1414 is well-documented for Europe, with rich archival sources for the Council of Constance and the Hundred Years' War. Chinese sources are comprehensive and official for Zheng He's voyages. Ottoman sources are less abundant but still substantial. African, American, and Oceanic sources are sparse or absent from European-trained historiography; oral traditions and archaeological evidence are the primary sources for these regions. The Inca chronology remains contested due to the absence of written records and reliance on oral traditions recorded by Spanish colonizers decades after the fact. European sources may overrepresent the significance of European events relative to events in other regions.

graph_edges.note: Council of Constance proceedings lead to trial and execution of John Hus
Council of Constance concludes with election of Pope Martin V, ending Western Schism
English military campaigns in France culminate in Battle of Agincourt
Portuguese exploration program leads to conquest of Ceuta
